options.name: Azure Container Apps serverless GPUs (Consumption GPU workload profile) + ACA Jobs
options.description: Queue-triggered ACA Job (KEDA azure-storage-queue/Service Bus scaler) running the GPU pipeline in the SAME resource group/environment family as the existing backend. Serverless T4/A100, scale-to-zero, per-second billing, works with Jobs (MS Learn doc updated 2026-05-02). T4 available in westus2 (your region); serverless A100 NOT in westus2 per the docs region table — use westus or westus3 for A100. Quota enabled by default for PAYG/EA accounts (otherwise support-case quota request). Caveat: 1 GPU per replica, no fractional GPUs; pricing calculator does not show GPU meters — verified via Azure Retail Prices API.
options.gpu_types: NVIDIA T4 (westus2: $0.000073/GPU-sec = $0.263/hr) and A100 (westus3: $0.000529/GPU-sec = $1.904/hr; westus: $0.000688/sec = $2.48/hr), plus vCPU-active ($0.000034/sec westus2 = $0.1224/vCPU-hr) and memory-active ($0.000004/GiB-sec = $0.0144/GiB-hr) billed additively
options.pricing: Azure Retail Prices API, queried live 2026-06-10 (meters effective Jan-Apr 2026): westus2 'Standard NC T4 v3 GPU Usage' $0.000073/sec; westus2 'Standard NC A100 v4 GPU Usage' meter exists at $0.000529/sec but docs (updated May 2026) list A100 serverless only in westus/westus3, not westus2; westus3 T4 $0.000073/sec + cheaper vCPU $0.000024/sec
options.est_cost_per_match: T4 westus2, job sized 4 vCPU/16 GiB: ($0.263 + 4×$0.1224 + 16×$0.0144) = $0.98/hr × 3.15 hr = ~$3.10/match (assumes 12.5 fps effective on 135k frames + 10 min overhead). Worst case at full 8 vCPU/56 GiB profile: $2.05/hr → ~$6.45/match. A100 in westus3 (8 vCPU/32 GiB): $2.94/hr × 0.95 hr = ~$2.80/match. So $2.80-6.45 depending on GPU/sizing; plan on ~$3/match
options.integration_effort: LOWEST. Already on ACA in westus2: add a workload-profile environment with Consumption-GPU T4 profile, deploy pipeline image as an event-driven ACA Job triggered by a Storage Queue/Service Bus message that the existing Flask app enqueues after upload; job reads/writes the same Blob account over the Azure backbone (no egress). Reuses existing ACR, managed identity, GitHub Actions deploy, log analytics. Roughly days, not weeks

options.name: Azure ML batch endpoints (or Azure Batch) on NC-series Spot VMs
options.description: AML batch endpoint with a GPU compute cluster (min nodes 0, scale-to-zero) running a scoring script per match; or raw Azure Batch pool. Batch endpoints themselves are free — you pay only underlying VMs. Supports Spot; AML auto-requeues tasks lost to preemption. IMPORTANT: AML Low-Priority VMs retire 2026-03-31 (already passed) — must use Spot VMs (MS Q&A + MS Learn 'Use low priority VMs in batch deployments', confirmed June 2026 searches). Cheapest per match by far, but preemption means non-deterministic completion times unless you fall back to on-demand.
options.gpu_types: NC8as_T4_v3 (1× T4, 8 vCPU/56 GiB), NC24ads_A100_v4 (1× A100 80GB), NV-series A10 fractions
options.pricing: Azure Retail Prices API westus2, queried 2026-06-10: NC8as_T4_v3 $0.752/hr on-demand, $0.218/hr Spot; NC4as_T4_v3 $0.526/$0.153; NC24ads_A100_v4 $3.673/hr on-demand, $1.039/hr Spot (~72% off); NV6ads_A10_v5 $0.454/$0.084
options.est_cost_per_match: T4 Spot (NC8as_T4_v3 $0.218/hr × 3.15 hr) = ~$0.69/match; T4 on-demand ~$2.37. A100 Spot ($1.039/hr × 0.95 hr) = ~$0.99/match; A100 on-demand ~$3.49. Best unit economics: ~$0.70-1.00/match on Spot, ~$2.40-3.50 on-demand fallback
options.integration_effort: MEDIUM-HIGH. New AML workspace + compute cluster + scoring/job script + invoking batch endpoint from Flask via azure-ai-ml SDK or REST; blob datastores map cleanly to existing storage. More moving parts (environments, MLflow-style packaging) and Spot preemption/retry handling. 1-2 weeks. Azure Batch variant is similar effort with lower-level control

options.name: Modal (third-party serverless GPU)
options.description: Python-native serverless: decorate pipeline function, modal deploy; trigger from Flask via Modal REST/web endpoint or modal client lib. True scale-to-zero, per-second billing, excellent batch DX (spawn/map, volumes for model weights). Data must leave Azure: pull video from Blob via short-lived read SAS inside the Modal function (Azure egress applies), write results back via write SAS. $30/mo free credits. SOC2; check customer data-residency expectations for match video.
options.integration_effort: LOW-MEDIUM. No Azure infra changes; Flask calls a Modal endpoint with blob SAS URLs, Modal posts a completion webhook back. Fastest path to a working pipeline (~days) but adds an external vendor, cross-cloud transfer per match, and data leaves the Azure boundary

options.name: RunPod Serverless
options.description: Per-second serverless workers (flex workers scale to zero); cheapest raw $/GPU-hr of the third parties. Less polished DX than Modal (build worker image + handler, queue-based API). Same cross-cloud egress + data-residency caveats as Modal. Community-cloud capacity can be variable; Secure Cloud costs more.
options.pricing: docs.runpod.io/serverless/pricing fetched 2026-06-10; billed per second from worker start to termination
options.est_cost_per_match: L4 @ ~22 fps effective: 1.7 hr × $0.684 = $1.16 + $0.44 Azure egress = ~$1.60/match. RTX 4090 @ ~28 fps: 1.35 hr × $1.12 = $1.51 + $0.44 = ~$1.95/match. A100: 0.95 hr × $2.74 + $0.44 = ~$3.05/match. Cheapest serverless option (~$1.60 on L4)
options.integration_effort: MEDIUM. Build RunPod worker (handler.py + Docker image), call run/runsync REST API from Flask with SAS URLs, poll or webhook for completion. More DIY reliability engineering (worker health, queue retries) than Modal

options.name: Replicate / Baseten (for completeness — not recommended for this workload)
options.description: Replicate: T4 $0.000225/s ($0.81/hr), A100-80 $0.0014/s ($5.04/hr) (replicate.com pricing via June 2026 search); but private models/deployments bill for ALL online time (setup+idle+active) and the platform is tuned for sub-minute predictions, not 1-3 hr batch jobs — poor fit. Baseten: per-minute dedicated deployments, scale-to-zero — T4 $0.01052/min ($0.63/hr), A10G $0.02012/min ($1.21/hr), A100 $4.00/hr (baseten.co/pricing via June 2026 search); inference-platform ergonomics (Truss) are aimed at request/response model serving, awkward for multi-hour video jobs.
options.pricing: Replicate per-second (June 2026): T4 $0.000225/s, A100-80 $0.0014/s; Baseten per-minute (June 2026): T4 $0.63/hr, A10G $1.21/hr, A100 $4.00/hr
options.est_cost_per_match: Baseten T4: 3.15 hr × $0.63 = $1.98 + $0.44 egress = ~$2.40/match; A10G ~$2.10/match. Replicate T4: ~$2.55 + egress = ~$3.00/match but idle/setup billing on deployments and long-job limits make real costs higher and the fit poor
options.integration_effort: MEDIUM-HIGH for this shape of workload; both are optimized for model-serving APIs, not 1-3 hour batch video jobs. Skip

recommended: RECOMMENDED: Azure Container Apps serverless GPU (T4) via event-driven ACA Jobs in westus2 — ~$3.10/match. Reasons: (1) zero new vendors and minimal new infra — you are already deployed on ACA in this region, and T4 serverless is available in westus2 (A100 is not; use westus3 if you later want A100 serverless); (2) video never leaves Azure, so no egress cost or data-residency questions for clubs' match footage (relevant for the Forest academy relationship); (3) scale-to-zero per-second billing matches a lumpy weekend-heavy workload; (4) queue-triggered Jobs give you retry semantics and parallel match processing for free. At a $20-25/match price point, $3/match COGS is an 85%+ gross margin, so the cheaper-but-heavier options are not worth the complexity for v1. FALLBACK / scale-up path: Azure ML batch endpoints on NC24ads_A100_v4 Spot ($1.039/hr westus2, ~$0.99/match) or NC8as_T4_v3 Spot (~$0.69/match) once volume exceeds ~100 matches/month — cuts GPU COGS ~70% and stays inside Azure; note AML Low-Priority VMs retired 2026-03-31, so build on Spot from day one. If Azure GPU quota or capacity ever blocks you, Modal A10 (~$2.35/match incl. egress) is the best external escape hatch given its days-not-weeks integration. Engineering note that dominates all pricing: decode with NVDEC (PyAVION/ffmpeg hwaccel or DALI), batch frames into the detector (TensorRT/FP16 YOLO), run homography keypoints at 1-5 Hz not per-frame, and consider processing at 12.5 fps (every other frame) with track interpolation — that alone halves every number above.
upload_storage_pattern: Browser → Azure Blob direct upload via SAS; never proxy multi-GB video through the Flask container. Flow: (1) Flask endpoint creates the match record and mints a short-lived (15-60 min) user-delegation SAS scoped to a single blob path (raw/{org}/{match_id}.mp4) with create+write only, via the backend's managed identity; (2) browser uses @azure/storage-blob BlockBlobClient — stageBlock/commitBlockList with 8-32 MiB blocks and concurrency 4-6 (uploadData handles this automatically via blockSize/concurrency options). Block blobs support up to 50,000 blocks / ~190.7 TiB, so a 6 GB file in 16 MiB blocks is ~375 blocks. Resumability: blocks are staged independently — on failure/reconnect, re-list uncommitted blocks (or track staged block IDs in localStorage/DB) and re-stage only missing ones, then commit; only the commitBlockList finalizes the blob (MS Learn performance-tuning doc + June 2026 best-practice sources). (3) On commit, client confirms to Flask (or use Event Grid BlobCreated) → Flask enqueues a processing message → GPU job picks it up. Storage costs (Azure Retail Prices API westus2, 2026-06-10, LRS): Hot $0.0184/GB-mo, Cool $0.01, Cold $0.0036, Archive $0.00099; write ops $0.05/10k (negligible: <$0.01/match). A 5 GB match = $0.09/mo Hot. Lifecycle policy: Hot on upload → Cool after 7-30 days (results stay Hot) → Cold/Archive at 90 days or delete per retention promise; amortized storage ≈ $0.10-0.20/match-season. Egress only applies when customers download video (5 GB out ≈ $0.44 at $0.087/GB after the free 100 GB/mo; result JSON/clips are ~$0.02-0.10/match) — serve analysis JSON + short clips, not full re-downloads. Azure Media Services was retired 2024-06-30 with NO first-party replacement — Microsoft points to partners (Ravnur, MediaKind MK.IO, Bitmovin) for streaming/DRM; for this CV pipeline you don't need any of them — decode in-job with ffmpeg/NVDEC, and if you later need browser-playable proxies, transcode a 720p H.264 preview in the same GPU job (NVENC is effectively free alongside inference).
cost_per_match_estimate: All-in COGS per 90-min 1080p25 match (135k frames), recommended path (ACA serverless T4, westus2): GPU+CPU+RAM ~$3.10 (3.15 hr × $0.98/hr at 4 vCPU/16 GiB; up to $6.45 if you size the full 8 vCPU/56 GiB profile) + storage ~$0.10-0.20 amortized with lifecycle tiering + result egress ~$0.05-0.10 + queue/ops/logging ~$0.05 ≈ $3.30-3.50/match (call it $3.50). On the Spot fallback (A100 NC24ads_A100_v4 Spot $1.039/hr × 0.95 hr): ≈ $1.10-1.30 all-in. Customer pricing: charge $25/match single, with credit packs at $20/match (10-pack = $200) and $15/match for 50+ packs/club-season deals — comparable sports-video analytics products (Veo, Trace, Spiideo per-match analysis) sit in the $25-75/match band, so $25 is competitive. Margins: 86% gross at $25 vs $3.50 COGS (v1), 78% even at the $15 bulk tier; on the Spot path margins exceed 90%, leaving room for re-processing, failed-job retries (budget ~5%), and support. Floor price: never sell below ~$8/match to keep 50%+ margin under worst-case sizing ($6.45). Credits also pre-fund the GPU spend (cash up front before COGS are incurred), which matters because each job has real marginal cost.
billing_pattern: Credit packs sold via Stripe Checkout one-time payments, with the credit ledger in your own Postgres — this is the simplest and the standard pattern for high-COGS per-job AI products (Stripe's own guidance: credits fit businesses where 'each usage event carries a substantial fixed cost' and provide up-front cash flow — stripe.com resources on credits-based models, June 2026). Implementation: products 'Single match' ($25), '10-pack' ($200), '50-pack' ($750) as one-time Prices; on checkout.session.completed webhook (the app already handles Stripe webhooks for Connect/subscriptions) increment a match_credits balance on the org; decrement atomically when a processing job is enqueued, refund the credit on hard job failure. Phase 0 even simpler: one Checkout Session per match (pay-then-process) — fine for the first handful of customers, no ledger needed. AVOID metered/usage-based subscription billing (Stripe Meters + invoice in arrears) for this: it bills after you've already burned GPU dollars, creating non-payment exposure on real COGS, and adds meter-event plumbing for no benefit at per-match granularity. If you later want Stripe to own the ledger, Stripe's native 'billing credits for usage-based billing' (GA, docs.stripe.com/billing/subscriptions/usage-based/billing-credits) supports prepaid credit grants drawn down by usage, but it requires the subscription/meter machinery — defer until enterprise club contracts need monthly invoicing with committed credit grants.
